title: Best Smart TVs 2026
excerpt: Smart TVs in 2026 deliver stunning 4K and 8K pictures with built-in streaming. We tested the 6 best smart TVs for picture quality, smart features, and value.
intro:
Smart TVs have evolved from simple connected displays into full-featured home entertainment platforms, integrating streaming services, voice assistants, gaming modes, and smart home control into a single screen. In 2026, OLED panels deliver perfect blacks and infinite contrast for cinephiles, QLED and Mini LED backlighting push brightness to new heights for HDR performance in bright rooms, and budget options from TCL and Hisense bring 4K quality to price points that were unthinkable five years ago.

The dominant smart TV operating systems — Samsung Tizen, LG webOS, Google TV (used by Sony and TCL), Amazon Fire TV, and Roku TV — each offer comprehensive streaming app libraries with differences in interface design, voice assistant integration, and gaming features. Samsung's Game Hub, LG's Game Optimizer, and Sony's BRAVIA Game Mode address the growing crossover between TV viewing and console gaming, with HDMI 2.1 ports supporting 4K/120Hz gaming on PS5 and Xbox Series X.

We evaluated smart TVs across picture quality in dark and bright environments, smart platform ease of use, gaming performance, HDR format support, and overall value. Here are the six best smart TVs for 2026.

products.name: Samsung 65" QN90C Neo QLED 4K Smart TV
pros: Mini LED backlighting with Neural Quantum Processor for exceptional HDR
Anti-Reflection coating dramatically reduces glare in bright living rooms
4 HDMI 2.1 ports support 4K/144Hz gaming on PC and consoles
Tizen OS has the most comprehensive app library of any smart TV platform
Dolby Atmos and Object Tracking Sound+ for immersive audio
cons: Premium pricing compared to OLED alternatives at similar screen sizes
Bloatware ads on home screen require manual removal in settings

products.name: LG 55" C3 OLED 4K Smart TV
pros: OLED panel delivers perfect blacks and infinite contrast ratio
a9 AI Processor Gen6 optimizes picture quality automatically per scene
4 HDMI 2.1 ports with 4K/120Hz and VRR for console gaming
webOS 23 is intuitive with personalized content recommendations
Dolby Vision IQ and HDR10 Pro format support
cons: OLED panels can experience burn-in with static content over many years
Peak brightness lower than top Mini LED panels in very bright rooms

products.name: Sony 65" BRAVIA XR X90L Full Array LED 4K TV
pros: Cognitive Processor XR replicates how human eyes see for natural picture
Google TV built-in with seamless integration of streaming and live TV
BRAVIA CAM (sold separately) enables auto picture optimization by position
Acoustic Multi-Audio sound fills room from speakers behind screen
Works natively with PlayStation 5 for auto game mode and VRR
cons: Google TV interface can feel cluttered compared to simpler Roku or Tizen
Full array LED blacks not as deep as OLED alternatives

products.name: TCL 65" S5 5-Series 4K QLED Google TV

Google TV provides full access to all major streaming apps
Game Master Pro 2.0 with 144Hz VRR for smooth gaming experience
Hands-free voice control via Google Assistant built in
AiPQ Engine processor optimizes picture scene by scene

products.name: Amazon Fire TV 55" Omni QLED Series 4K
pros: Alexa built-in for hands-free voice control of TV and smart home
Adaptive brightness adjusts picture automatically to room lighting
Dolby Vision IQ and HDR10 Plus Adaptive HDR formats

Screen Mirroring and AirPlay 2 compatible for iPhone and Android

Gaming features less developed than Samsung or LG equivalents

pros: 3000 nit peak brightness is among the highest of any consumer TV
Mini LED with 1000+ local dimming zones for excellent contrast
144Hz native panel with VRR support for PC and console gaming
Google TV for full streaming app access including Netflix and Disney+
Dolby Vision, HDR10+, and HLG all supported
cons: Hisense brand carries less prestige resale value than Samsung or Sony
Menu UI slightly less polished than first-party Google TV implementations

buying_guide.title: OLED vs. QLED vs. Mini LED: Which Panel Technology Is Right for You?
buying_guide.body: Panel technology is the single most important decision when buying a smart TV in 2026. OLED (Organic Light Emitting Diode) panels, used by LG, Sony, and Samsung in their premium lines, allow each pixel to emit and switch off its own light independently — the result is perfect blacks, infinite contrast ratio, and virtually no blooming of bright objects against dark backgrounds. OLED is the gold standard for dark-room movie watching and is why cinephiles consistently recommend LG C-series or Sony A-series OLEDs. The tradeoff: OLED panels have lower peak brightness than LED alternatives, so bright HDR highlights don't pop as dramatically in a sunlit room, and long-term static image retention (burn-in) is a real risk for users who frequently display static game UI or news tickers. QLED is Samsung's marketing term for LED TVs with quantum dot color filters — they produce wider color gamuts and higher brightness than standard LED TVs but rely on LED backlighting with local dimming zones, so blacks are not as deep as OLED. Mini LED is an evolution of QLED that uses thousands of smaller LED zones for more precise dimming — Samsung Neo QLED and Hisense ULED are the leaders here, with peak brightness and HDR performance that can match or exceed OLED in bright rooms while costing less. For bright living rooms or sports viewing, a top Mini LED panel often outperforms OLED in real-world conditions. For dedicated home theaters, OLED remains unmatched.

buying_guide.title: Smart TV Operating Systems Compared
buying_guide.body: The smart TV operating system determines your daily interface experience, app availability, and voice assistant integration. Samsung Tizen OS has the most comprehensive streaming app library of any platform and integrates Samsung SmartThings for home automation, but its home screen fills with sponsored content by default. LG webOS is widely praised for its intuitive floating quick-launch bar design, smooth navigation, and Magic Remote with point-and-click control — it's the easiest to use for less tech-savvy households. Google TV (found in Sony and TCL) aggregates content from all streaming apps into a single personalized feed with Google Assistant integration, making it excellent for households using Google services, but it can feel cluttered. Amazon Fire TV is optimized for Prime Video users and Alexa integration — the interface prioritizes Amazon content. Roku TV (found in budget Hisense, TCL, and Philips models) is the simplest and most channel-agnostic platform with a clean channel-grid layout. All platforms support Netflix, Disney+, Hulu, HBO Max, and YouTube in 2026 — the differences come down to app depth, interface philosophy, and ecosystem alignment.

buying_guide.title: Screen Size: How to Choose the Right TV for Your Room
buying_guide.body: Screen size selection depends on viewing distance and room size. A commonly cited guideline for 4K TVs is to multiply your desired screen diagonal by 1.5 to get the minimum comfortable viewing distance in inches — a 65-inch TV needs at least 97 inches (about 8 feet) of viewing distance for comfortable viewing. In practice, most living rooms work well with: 55 inches for rooms where the couch is 7-9 feet from the TV, 65 inches for the most common living room size at 9-11 feet distance, 75 inches for large living rooms at 12+ feet, and 85-inch-plus for dedicated media rooms or large open-plan spaces. The key insight: with 4K resolution, you can sit considerably closer than was recommended with 1080p HD TVs without seeing pixelation — this makes going up one size larger a genuine visual improvement rather than overkill. Wall mounting makes larger screens more practical because the screen is closer to the wall, reducing the visual intrusion. If you're replacing a TV you currently enjoy and want a meaningful upgrade, going one full size step up (55 to 65, or 65 to 75) is often more impactful than switching panel technology on the same size.

buying_guide.title: Gaming Features: What to Look for in a TV for PS5 and Xbox
buying_guide.body: Gaming on a modern smart TV has become a first-class use case, with manufacturers adding dedicated gaming modes that reduce input lag and enable variable refresh rates. The key gaming specs to look for are: HDMI 2.1 ports (required for 4K/120Hz gaming on PS5 and Xbox Series X — most budget TVs only have HDMI 2.0 which caps at 4K/60Hz), VRR (Variable Refresh Rate) support in FreeSync or G-Sync compatible form, ALLM (Auto Low Latency Mode) which automatically switches to game mode when a console is detected, and input lag below 15ms in game mode (measured at 4K/60Hz). LG OLED C3 is consistently rated the best gaming TV due to OLED's near-instantaneous pixel response, 4 HDMI 2.1 ports, and around 1ms input lag in game mode. Samsung Neo QLED models feature 4K/144Hz support via HDMI 2.1, giving PC gamers a significant advantage over consoles' 120Hz cap. TV-based gaming is also expanding beyond consoles: most 2026 smart TVs include app-based cloud gaming through GeForce Now, Xbox Cloud Gaming, or Amazon Luna, requiring only a Bluetooth controller and no console hardware.

buying_guide.title: Sound Quality and When to Buy a Soundbar
buying_guide.body: TV audio has improved incrementally but still represents the weakest link in most smart TV setups. Modern slim TVs have limited internal speaker volume and are physically unable to produce deep bass frequencies — sound comes from downward-firing or rear-firing speakers that often bounce off the TV stand or cabinet with muffled results. Premium TVs address this with larger driver arrays: Sony Acoustic Multi-Audio uses speaker arrays near the screen edges for sound that appears to originate from the action; Samsung Q-Symphony synchronizes TV speakers with a matched soundbar for a more enveloping experience. For casual news, streaming, and background TV watching, built-in speakers are adequate. For movies, sports, and music listening, even a budget 2.1 soundbar (such as the Sonos Ray or Samsung HW-B43M) dramatically improves the listening experience by adding forward-facing speakers and actual subwoofer bass. The 4K picture quality of modern TVs so thoroughly outpaces the audio that upgrading both picture and sound simultaneously — buying a midrange TV and allocating $200-300 toward a soundbar — often delivers a better complete experience than spending the entire budget on the screen alone.

buying_guide.title: HDR Formats Explained: Dolby Vision, HDR10+, and HLG
buying_guide.body: HDR (High Dynamic Range) content formats determine whether your TV can display the brighter highlights and deeper shadows encoded into premium streaming content. HDR10 is the universal baseline format supported by all HDR TVs and all streaming services — if a TV supports HDR, it supports HDR10. HDR10+ is Samsung's enhanced format that adds dynamic scene-by-scene metadata to optimize HDR tone mapping throughout a film — supported on Samsung, Panasonic, TCL, and Hisense TVs and available on Amazon Prime Video and some Disney+ content. Dolby Vision is the competing premium format from Dolby that uses 12-bit color and scene-by-scene dynamic metadata — widely considered the superior HDR experience and supported by LG, Sony, TCL, and others, with the broadest streaming support across Netflix, Disney+, Apple TV+, and Vudu. HLG (Hybrid Log-Gamma) is the broadcast standard for live HDR content from cable and satellite providers. The practical advice: if you subscribe to Netflix or Apple TV+, choosing a TV with Dolby Vision support ensures you'll see the best HDR version of their premium content. Samsung's own TVs deliberately omit Dolby Vision support — choosing Samsung means relying on HDR10+ for premium HDR, which is well-implemented on Samsung panels but has less streaming platform support.

faq.q: What is the best smart TV brand in 2026?
faq.a: The best smart TV brand depends on your priorities. Samsung leads overall with the broadest model lineup, most comprehensive smart platform (Tizen), and top-tier Mini LED technology in its Neo QLED range — the QN90C is a strong all-rounder. LG is the best brand for pure picture quality through its OLED C-series, which consistently earns top marks from display critics and is the preferred choice for home theater enthusiasts. Sony's BRAVIA line offers the best balance of picture quality and smart platform (Google TV) and has unique PlayStation integration. TCL and Hisense have dramatically closed the gap with premium brands in picture quality while maintaining budget-friendly pricing, making them the best value propositions in 2026. For most households, a Samsung QN90C or LG C3 OLED represents the best combination of features, picture quality, and long-term software support from brands with proven track records.

faq.q: How long do smart TVs last?
faq.a: Smart TVs typically last 7-10 years in terms of hardware reliability — the physical panel, backlight, and circuit board components are durable. The limiting factor is software support: most manufacturers provide 3-5 years of operating system updates and new app compatibility before older TV models are no longer updated. After software support ends, apps may stop working (Netflix has already dropped support for older Samsung and LG TV models), and new streaming formats may not be supported. OLED panel longevity is a genuine question: LG rates its OLED panels for 100,000 hours of half-brightness life (equivalent to watching 8 hours per day for 34 years), but panel brightness degrades gradually over time and varies by usage pattern. Mini LED and QLED LED backlights are rated for similar or longer longevity. To maximize smart TV lifespan, keep the software updated when available, enable screen savers or automatic shut-off timers, and avoid prolonged static content display on OLED panels.

8K TVs are not worth buying for most consumers in 2026. The fundamental problem is content: there is virtually no native 8K streaming content available on any major platform, 8K Blu-ray does not exist as a consumer format, and even the most advanced gaming hardware tops out at 4K/120fps rather than 8K. Samsung, LG, and Sony sell 8K TVs and use AI upscaling to make 4K and lower-resolution content look sharper on the 8K panel, but independent testing consistently shows that the perceptual improvement over a top-tier 4K TV is marginal at normal viewing distances — the human eye cannot reliably distinguish 4K from 8K resolution on a 65-75 inch screen from 8-10 feet away. The price premium for 8K (typically 2-4x the cost of an equivalent 4K model) is difficult to justify given the content ecosystem and perceptual reality. Spend the same money on a larger 4K OLED or top-tier Mini LED TV and you will get a far more impactful visual improvement.

faq.q: What screen size TV should I get for a living room?
faq.a: For typical living rooms where the sofa is 8-12 feet from the TV, 65 inches is the sweet spot in 2026 — prices have dropped significantly, and the extra screen real estate over 55 inches is immediately noticeable without being overwhelming. If your room is larger with the primary viewing distance at 12+ feet, a 75-inch TV becomes appropriate. The old rule of thumb that TVs shouldn't dominate a room has reversed as screen quality improved — people now consistently report that they wish they had bought a size larger after installation. A practical approach: use blue painter's tape to mock up the exact dimensions of the TV on your wall and live with it for a day before ordering. For rooms smaller than 12 feet wide with the TV on a central wall, 55 inches prevents screen fatigue. Most TV shoppers in 2026 are choosing between 65 and 75 inches, and the price difference has narrowed enough that 75 inches is often the better long-term investment.

faq.q: Does a smart TV need a streaming device if it has built-in apps?
faq.a: Whether you need an external streaming device with a built-in smart TV depends on your satisfaction with the TV's built-in platform. Modern smart TV platforms (especially Samsung Tizen, LG webOS, and Google TV) are capable enough for most users and include all major streaming apps. Reasons to add an external device like a Roku Streaming Stick, Amazon Fire TV Stick, or Apple TV 4K: your TV's built-in platform receives infrequent updates and runs slowly, you prefer a specific platform (Apple TV 4K is the only way to get tvOS on non-Apple hardware), you want Dolby Vision and Atmos support on a TV that otherwise lacks it, or you want to upgrade a non-smart TV. For newer 2024-2026 smart TVs, the built-in platform is typically sufficient for at least 3-4 years before performance degradation becomes a practical issue. Apple TV 4K remains worth the premium for households deeply integrated with iPhone, iPad, and Mac — its hardware consistently outperforms TV-embedded smart platforms in speed and app availability.

verdict: The Samsung 65-inch QN90C Neo QLED at $1,197.99 is our Best Overall pick for 2026, delivering exceptional HDR performance through Mini LED backlighting, anti-reflection coating for bright living rooms, and 4 HDMI 2.1 ports supporting 4K/144Hz gaming. Its Tizen OS offers the most comprehensive app library of any smart TV platform. For viewers who prioritize pure picture quality over brightness, the LG 55-inch C3 OLED at $1,099.99 is the stronger runner-up: its pixel-level light control produces perfect blacks and infinite contrast that no LED panel can match, making it the definitive choice for dedicated home theaters and dark-room movie watching. Budget shoppers should consider the TCL 65-inch S5 at $429.99, which brings QLED color and Google TV to a price that was unthinkable five years ago.
